edges.description: COG1 splice or truncating defects can delay retrograde trafficking in patient fibroblasts.
edges.source: COG1 Deficiency Disrupts the Conserved Oligomeric Golgi Complex

edges.description: COG-complex disruption alters Golgi glycosylation-enzyme localization, causing combined N- and O-glycosylation defects.
edges.source: COG1 Deficiency Disrupts the Conserved Oligomeric Golgi Complex

edges.description: Retrograde trafficking abnormalities impair maintenance of Golgi glycosylation machinery, producing the combined glycosylation defect.
Combined glycosylation impairment produces abnormal serum N-glycan sialylation/galactosylation and abnormal mucin-type O-glycan sialylation.
edges.target: Abnormal serum N- and O-glycan processing

edges.description: Defective Golgi glycosylation can be detected clinically as a type II transferrin isoform profile.
Orphanet records failure to thrive as part of the multisystem COG1-CDG phenotype spectrum.
COG1-CDG glycosylation defects are associated with developmental delay and seizures or convulsions in reported patients.
COG1-CDG glycosylation defects are associated with dysmorphism in a reported neonatal presentation.
COG1-CDG glycosylation defects are associated with strabismus in a reported compound heterozygous case.
Reported patients can show hepatitis as a hepatic manifestation of COG1-CDG.
Reported patients can show hypoglycemia as a metabolic manifestation.
A COG1 splice-variant presentation included costovertebral dysplasia as a skeletal manifestation.
Developmental retardation is one of the main reported clinical symptoms.
Seizures or convulsions are reported neurologic manifestations.
Dysmorphism is reported in the neonatal COG1-CDG presentation.
Orphanet records Pierre-Robin sequence as a frequent craniofacial phenotype of COG1-CDG.
Strabismus is reported among the main symptoms in a COG1-CDG case.
Hepatitis is a reported hepatic manifestation.
Hypoglycemia is reported as a main symptom and was responsive to glucose infusion in one case.
Costovertebral dysplasia is the reported skeletal manifestation in the two COG1 c.1070+5G>A patients.
Orphanet records posterior rib gap as a frequent skeletal phenotype of COG1-CDG.
Orphanet records butterfly vertebrae as a frequent vertebral phenotype of COG1-CDG.
A type II transferrin pattern reports abnormal Golgi-stage glycan processing, especially altered serum glycoprotein sialylation.
edges.source: Abnormal serum N- and O-glycan processing
edges.target: Type II serum transferrin isoelectric focusing pattern

edges.description: Combined N- and O-glycosylation abnormalities report the Golgi processing defect caused by COG1-complex dysfunction.

edges.target: COG1 Deficiency Disrupts the Conserved Oligomeric Golgi Complex

nodes.description: Patient serum and lectin assays show reduced sialic acid and galactose on N-glycans and reduced sialylation of core 1 mucin-type O-glycans, producing the type II CDG biochemical signature.
nodes.id: Abnormal serum N- and O-glycan processing

evidence.snippet: Mass spectrometric analysis of the structures of the N-linked glycans released from glycoproteins from the patient's serum revealed a reduction in sialic acid and galactose residues.
Peanut agglutinin (PNA) lectin staining revealed a decrease in sialic acids on core 1 mucin type O-glycans, indicating a combined defect in N- and O-glycosylation.

nodes.description: Butterfly vertebrae are recorded as a frequent vertebral phenotype of COG1-CDG in Orphanet.

evidence.snippet: HP:0003316 | Butterfly vertebrae | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Pathogenic COG1 variants impair function of the conserved oligomeric Golgi complex, disrupting intra-Golgi trafficking and the localization or stability of Golgi glycosylation enzymes.
nodes.id: COG1 Deficiency Disrupts the Conserved Oligomeric Golgi Complex

evidence.snippet: This mutation destabilizes several other COG subunits and alters their subcellular localization and hence the overall integrity of the COG complex.

Sequence analysis of the COG1 cDNA and gene identified a homozygous insertion of a single nucleotide (2659–2660insC), which is predicted to lead to a premature translation stop and truncation of the C terminus of the Cog1 protein by 80 amino acids.
Exome sequencing identified a homozygous COG1 variant (NM_018714.3: c.2665dup: p.[Arg889Profs*12]), which has been reported previously in one patient.
The c.2492G > A (p. Arg831Gln) mutation in exon 10 of the COG1 gene may be a potential pathogenetic variant for CDG-IIg.
This patient has a defect in both N- and O-glycosylation.

readouts.interpretation: Combined N- and O-glycosylation abnormalities report the Golgi processing defect caused by COG1-complex dysfunction.

nodes.description: Costovertebral dysplasia was reported in two COG1 c.1070+5G>A patients with a cerebrocostomandibular-like presentation, suggesting a skeletal phenotype in this mutation class.

evidence.snippet: The costovertebral dysplasia of the two patients has been described in cerebrocostomandibular syndrome (CCMS), but also in cerebrofaciothoracic dysplasia and spondylocostal dysostosis.
We identified in both patients an intronic mutation, c.1070+5G>A, that disrupts a splice donor site and leads to skipping of exon 6, a frameshift and a premature stopcodon in exon 7.

nodes.description: Two COG1-mutated patients with the recurrent c.1070+5G>A splice variant were described with cerebrocostomandibular-like syndrome and costovertebral dysplasia, supporting a skeletal branch in this mutation class.

evidence.snippet: We describe two patients with a cerebrocostomandibular-like syndrome and a novel mutation in conserved oligomeric Golgi (COG) subunit 1
The costovertebral dysplasia of the two patients has been described in cerebrocostomandibular syndrome (CCMS), but also in cerebrofaciothoracic dysplasia and spondylocostal dysostosis.
We identified in both patients an intronic mutation, c.1070+5G>A, that disrupts a splice donor site and leads to skipping of exon 6, a frameshift and a premature stopcodon in exon 7.

nodes.description: Dysmorphism is reported as a craniofacial manifestation in COG1-CDG.

evidence.snippet: We report a male with neonatal seizures, dysmorphism, hepatitis and a type 2 serum transferrin isoelectrofocusing.

nodes.description: Disrupted Golgi trafficking impairs glycoprotein processing, producing a type II CDG pattern with combined N-linked and O-linked glycosylation abnormalities.

evidence.snippet: This patient has a defect in both N- and O-glycosylation.
Mass spectrometric analysis of the structures of the N-linked glycans released from glycoproteins from the patient's serum revealed a reduction in sialic acid and galactose residues.
Peanut agglutinin (PNA) lectin staining revealed a decrease in sialic acids on core 1 mucin type O-glycans, indicating a combined defect in N- and O-glycosylation.

nodes.description: Dysmorphic features are reported as part of the neonatal COG1-CDG presentation.

evidence.snippet: We report a male with neonatal seizures, dysmorphism, hepatitis and a type 2 serum transferrin isoelectrofocusing.

nodes.description: Failure to thrive is recorded as a frequent phenotype of COG1-CDG in Orphanet.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001508 | Failure to thrive | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Developmental delay is part of the reported COG1-CDG neurological phenotype.

evidence.snippet: The patient was male, and the main clinical symptoms were developmental retardation, convulsion, strabismus, and hypoglycemia, which is rarely reported in CDG-IIg.

nodes.description: COG1-CDG can include hepatitis alongside the type II glycosylation defect.

evidence.snippet: We report a male with neonatal seizures, dysmorphism, hepatitis and a type 2 serum transferrin isoelectrofocusing.

nodes.description: Hepatic involvement with hepatitis or elevated transaminases can occur in COG1-CDG.

evidence.snippet: We report a male with neonatal seizures, dysmorphism, hepatitis and a type 2 serum transferrin isoelectrofocusing.

nodes.description: Hypoglycemia has been reported in a COG1-CDG case and may require acute glucose treatment.

evidence.snippet: The patient was male, and the main clinical symptoms were developmental retardation, convulsion, strabismus, and hypoglycemia, which is rarely reported in CDG-IIg.

nodes.description: COG1-CDG can include hypoglycemia alongside the type II glycosylation defect.

evidence.snippet: The patient was male, and the main clinical symptoms were developmental retardation, convulsion, strabismus, and hypoglycemia, which is rarely reported in CDG-IIg.

nodes.description: The reported COG1-CDG neurological spectrum includes developmental delay and neonatal seizures or convulsions.

evidence.snippet: The patient was male, and the main clinical symptoms were developmental retardation, convulsion, strabismus, and hypoglycemia, which is rarely reported in CDG-IIg.
We report a male with neonatal seizures, dysmorphism, hepatitis and a type 2 serum transferrin isoelectrofocusing.

nodes.description: Strabismus is reported as an ocular alignment manifestation in COG1-CDG.

evidence.snippet: The patient was male, and the main clinical symptoms were developmental retardation, convulsion, strabismus, and hypoglycemia, which is rarely reported in CDG-IIg.

nodes.description: Pierre-Robin sequence is recorded as a frequent craniofacial phenotype of COG1-CDG in Orphanet.

nodes.description: Posterior rib gap is recorded as a frequent skeletal phenotype of COG1-CDG in Orphanet.

evidence.snippet: HP:0030282 | Posterior rib gap | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: COG1 deficiency impairs retrograde vesicular trafficking through the Golgi, disrupting the trafficking environment needed to maintain glycosylation enzyme localization and glycan processing.

evidence.snippet: This hetero-octameric protein complex is involved in retrograde vesicular trafficking and glycosylation.
A delay in retrograde trafficking could be demonstrated by Brefeldin A treatment of this patient's fibroblasts.

nodes.description: Seizures, including neonatal seizures in one review case and convulsions in another case report, are reported neurological manifestations.

evidence.snippet: We report a male with neonatal seizures, dysmorphism, hepatitis and a type 2 serum transferrin isoelectrofocusing.

nodes.description: Strabismus was reported among the main symptoms in a compound heterozygous COG1-CDG case.

evidence.snippet: The patient was male, and the main clinical symptoms were developmental retardation, convulsion, strabismus, and hypoglycemia, which is rarely reported in CDG-IIg.

nodes.description: No disease-modifying therapy is established for COG1-CDG; management is supportive and directed at manifestations such as hypoglycemia and seizures.

evidence.snippet: We treated the patient with glucose infusion and he was recovered from hypoglycemia.

nodes.description: A type II transferrin isoform profile is recorded as a frequent biochemical phenotype of COG1-CDG in Orphanet.

evidence.snippet: HP:0012301 | Type II transferrin isoform profile | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.id: Type II serum transferrin isoelectric focusing pattern

evidence.snippet: We report a male with neonatal seizures, dysmorphism, hepatitis and a type 2 serum transferrin isoelectrofocusing.

readouts.interpretation: A type II transferrin pattern reports abnormal Golgi-stage glycan processing, especially altered serum glycoprotein sialylation.
